one_line_takeaway: A comprehensive, well-structured 102-study systematic review concluding that LLMs are a double-edged code-security tool - error-prone at generating and fixing secure code but useful for detection, especially when paired with static analysis and human review - while deliberately avoiding cross-study numeric comparison due to benchmark heterogeneity.
key_finding: The evidence is genuinely mixed and the survey resists a single verdict. LLMs frequently introduce vulnerabilities (injection appears in 17 of 21 generation studies; CWE-79 XSS and CWE-89 SQLi are the most recurrent), yet on relative terms may be safer than some human sources (Hamer et al.: ChatGPT code had ~20% fewer CWEs than Stack Overflow snippets; Asare et al.: Copilot less likely to be vulnerable than human developers). For detection, LLMs often match or beat static analysis tools but at much higher false-positive cost, and benchmark scores are inflated relative to realistic settings (StarCoder2-7B: 68.26% F1 on BigVul collapses to 3.09% on the harder PrimeVul benchmark). Hybrid LLM+SAT pipelines are the most reliable improvement lever (e.g., IRIS: +35% detections and -80% false positives over CodeQL alone). Fixing is the weakest capability: gains from fine-tuning and pre-processing are small and confined to simple, localized repairs, and reliable security fixing still requires human-in-the-loop validation. Multi-round re-prompting can mitigate many self-introduced vulnerabilities (Liu et al.: >89% mitigated) but LLMs exhibit 'self-repair blind spots' on their own code.
core_contribution: A systematic literature review (SLR) synthesizing 102 primary studies on the security implications of LLMs across the full code lifecycle. Its distinctive contribution (versus the prior surveys it tabulates in Related Work, Table 1) is being the first secondary study to jointly cover all four themes: (1) vulnerabilities introduced by LLM-generated code, (2) LLM vulnerability detection, (3) LLM vulnerability fixing, plus their unified detect+fix pipelines, and (4) the effect of prompting strategies and data poisoning on these tasks. Its most concrete original artifact is a new 10-category taxonomy of vulnerabilities found in LLM-generated code (Injection, Memory Management, File Management, Deserialization, Sensitive Data Exposure, Authentication/Authorization, Cryptography, Resource Management, Coding Standards, Error Handling), each mapped to specific CWE identifiers across 21 studies (Table 3). It also catalogs prompting techniques (Table 5) and non-LLM baselines (Table 4) used across the corpus.
threat_model: As an SLR the paper organizes, rather than proposes, a threat model. The threats it synthesizes are: (a) LLMs silently emitting insecure code that developers accept and ship (e.g., Pearce et al. found ~40% of GitHub Copilot outputs across 89 high-risk scenarios were vulnerable; Gong et al. found 75% of tested LLM code was insecure); (b) LLM detectors producing high false-positive rates (up to 63-97% for GPT-4 per Cetin et al.) or missing real-world vulnerabilities; (c) LLM fixes that are incomplete, non-deterministic, or hallucinated; and (d) training-data poisoning attacks, where an adversary injects malicious samples into the web-scraped corpora (GitHub, HuggingFace) used to train code LLMs, degrading secure-generation and detection ability. The review is explicitly scoped OUT of agent-based LLM systems (EX6) and smart-contract vulnerabilities (EX7).
limitations: By the authors' own account and by construction: (1) it is a secondary study, so all quantitative claims are inherited from primary studies of varying rigor, not independently measured; (2) the authors explicitly decline cross-study numerical comparison because datasets/metrics are inconsistent, which limits how quantitatively actionable the synthesis can be; (3) threats to validity include search-string and database coverage bias and subjective screening; (4) the corpus is heavily skewed toward C/C++ code and GPT-3.5/GPT-4 models (GPT models in 30 of 57 detection studies), so conclusions generalize weakly to other languages and model families; (5) several vulnerability categories (Deserialization, Resource Management, Error Handling) are flagged as under-studied with no paper focusing on them; (6) 2026 coverage is partial since the search ran in January 2026. NOTE for graders: this review's data-poisoning section (Sec. 9) and its explicit RQ answers / open-challenges (Sec. 10) and conclusion (Sec. 11) fall beyond the 22-page text extraction and were not directly read; poisoning claims here are drawn from the abstract, introduction, and Related Work only.

datasets_used: Note: these are benchmarks/datasets catalogued across the reviewed primary studies (Tables 6-9), NOT datasets the survey itself experiments on
SARD, NVD, Juliet, Devign, BigVul (recurring detection benchmarks)
PrimeVul (Ding et al., introduced as a harder, less-overestimated detection benchmark)
MVFSC (Carletti et al., 972 real-world C/C++ functions from production open-source projects)
ExtractFix, CVEFixes, Big-Vul, Vul4J (recurring vulnerability-fixing benchmarks)
DiverseVul (used in Qiu et al.'s RLV cross-project detection setting)
quantitative_results: Corpus: 7008 records -> 6072 deduplicated -> 102 included studies (96 primary + 6 snowballed)
10 vulnerability categories defined; 21 studies reported LLM-generated-code vulnerabilities; injection present in 17 papers
Detection subcorpus: 57 studies; GPT-3.5/GPT-4 in 30/57, Llama in 24, DeepSeek 13, Qwen 8, Mistral 7
Pearce et al.: ~40% of GitHub Copilot outputs across 89 high-risk scenarios were vulnerable
Liu et al.: 91.8% of vulnerabilities in ChatGPT code over 728 algorithmic problems were MissingNullTest (CWE-476)
Aydin et al.: CWE-200 was most prevalent in LLM-generated JavaScript across 6 LLMs (127 occurrences, 21% of reported vulns)
Hamer et al.: ChatGPT-generated code had ~20% fewer vulnerabilities than Stack Overflow snippets
Ding et al. (PrimeVul): StarCoder2-7B scored 68.26% F1 on BigVul but only 3.09% on PrimeVul
Lin et al.: detection F1 57.98% on Java vs 34.71% on C/C++; Khare et al.: 79% (Juliet Java) vs 70% (Juliet C/C++)
Wang et al. [111]: LLM+execution pipeline 95.07% F1 vs 78.36% best SAT baseline
Li et al. IRIS: +35% detected vulnerabilities and -80% false positives vs CodeQL alone
Munson et al.: Semgrep 74.15% F1 improved to 86.92% with Semgrep+o1-mini
Fixing - Braconaro et al.: GPT-4o 93.8% accuracy vs Gemini 1.5 Flash 83.8% / Gemini 83.5%; Le et al.: ChatGPT 71.66% vs Bard 68.33% repair accuracy
APPATCH (Nong et al.): 90% recall, 68.41% F1 on ExtractFix; SPVR (Wang et al.): GPT-4 repairs 65/547 -> 143/547 (pass@3)
Wu et al.: Codex fixed 20.4% of Java vulns off-the-shelf; de-Fitero-Dominguez et al.: Code Llama/Mistral 23%/26% Perfect Prediction after fine-tuning

Unified detect+fix - Liu et al.: >89% of vulnerabilities mitigated via multi-round re-prompting; Gong et al.: 75% of LLM code insecure, advanced LLMs fix up to 60% of others' insecure code but hit self-repair blind spots; Zhang et al. [127]: fine-tuned GPT-3.5 reached 98.82% F1 detecting code-injection defects but was ineffective at fixing them

False positives - Cetin et al.: GPT-4 63% FP (other models up to 97%); Ozturk et al.: ChatGPT detected 62-68% of vulns (best SAT 32%) with 91% FP; Chen et al. [16] VulKILLER-style pipeline reduced FP to 4.3%
baselines_compared: Note: baselines are those catalogued in the reviewed studies (Table 4), not run by the survey authors
Static analysis tools: CodeQL, SpotBugs, Fortify, Semgrep, SonarQube, Checkmarx, Flawfinder, RATS, Infer, Joern, Bandit, CryptoGuard, CogniCryptSAST, UBITect, Bearer, MobSFscan
Specialized ML/DL vulnerability detectors: VulDeePecker, SySeVR, Devign, ReVeal, LineVul, IVDetect, MAGNET, CFGNN, AMPLE
Pre-trained language models (treated as traditional-ML baselines): CodeBERT, GraphCodeBERT, UniXcoder, CodeT5/CodeT5P, PLBART, VulBERTa, ContraBERT, BERT-family models
Classical ML classifiers: Logistic Regression, Multinomial/Bernoulli Naive Bayes, Linear SVC, KNN, Decision Tree, Random Forest, MLP, CNN, BiLSTM
